title: Accessing Matrix Elements
description: Indexing matrices with row/column indices, logical conditions, and names.

slides.title: Matrix Indexing 🔍
slides.content:
You access matrix elements using square brackets with two indices: `matrix[row, col]`.

- To get a single element: `m[2, 3]` (row 2, column 3)
- To get an entire row: `m[1, ]`
- To get an entire column: `m[, 2]`
- To get a submatrix: `m[1:2, 2:3]`

If you omit an index, it means "all".

slides.title: Basic Indexing
slides.codeSnippet:
m <- matrix(1:12, nrow=3, ncol=4)
print(m)

print(m[2, 3])      # row 2, col 3
print(m[1, ])       # first row
print(m[, 2])       # second column
print(m[2:3, 3:4])  # rows 2-3, cols 3-4

quizData.question: Given a 3x3 matrix `m`, what does `m[2, ]` return?

options.text: The second row as a vector
The second column as a vector

quizData.explanation: When you select a single row, R returns a vector (dropping the dimensions) unless you use `drop=FALSE`.

slides.title: Preserving Dimensions with drop=FALSE
slides.content:
By default, selecting a single row or column returns a vector (dimensions dropped). To keep it as a matrix, use `drop=FALSE`.

```r
m[1, , drop=FALSE]   # 1x3 matrix
m[, 2, drop=FALSE]   # 3x1 matrix
```

slides.title: drop=FALSE
slides.codeSnippet:
m <- matrix(1:9, nrow=3)
print(m[1, ])
print(class(m[1, ]))

print(m[1, , drop=FALSE])
print(class(m[1, , drop=FALSE]))

slides.title: Indexing with Names
slides.content:
If rows and columns have names, you can use those:

```r
m <- matrix(1:4, nrow=2)
rownames(m) <- c("r1", "r2")
colnames(m) <- c("c1", "c2")
m["r1", "c2"]   # element at row1, col2
m["r2", ]       # second row
```

quizData.question: If `colnames(m)` are `c('A','B','C')` and `rownames(m)` are `c('X','Y')`, how do you get the element at row Y, column B?
quizData.explanation: Both m['Y','B'] and m[2,2] work, but the question asks how to use names, so m['Y','B'] is the answer.

slides.title: Logical Indexing
slides.content:
You can also use logical vectors to select rows or columns. The logical vector must have the same length as the number of rows (for row selection) or columns (for column selection).

```r
m <- matrix(1:12, nrow=4)
m[c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE), ]  # select rows 1 and 3
```

Or use conditions on the matrix elements themselves: `m[m > 5]` returns a vector of elements greater than 5.

slides.title: Logical Subsetting
slides.codeSnippet:
m <- matrix(1:12, nrow=3)
print(m[m > 5])

# Select rows where first column > 2
rows_keep <- m[,1] > 2
print(m[rows_keep, ])

slides.title: Matrix Indexing Practice
slides.content: Create a 4x4 matrix with numbers 1:16. Extract the submatrix of rows 2 and 4, columns 1 and 3. Then extract all elements greater than 8.
slides.codeSnippet:
m <- matrix(1:16, nrow=4)
sub <- m[c(2,4), c(1,3)]
print(sub)
gt8 <- m[m > 8]
print(gt8)
